name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَآ أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ ۖ قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ ۙ وَمَا عَلَّمْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّآ أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٤)
```

```english
They ask you as to what is lawful to them. Say, ‘All the good things are lawful to you.’ As for what you have taught hunting dogs [to catch], teaching them out of what Allah has taught you, eat of what they catch for you and mention Allah’s Name over it, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (4)
```

```arabic
ٱلْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ ۖ وَطَعَامُ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حِلٌّ لَّكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ ۖ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ إِذَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ وَلَا مُتَّخِذِىٓ أَخْدَانٍ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُۥ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٥)
```

```english
Today all the good things have been made lawful to you—the food of those who were given the Book is lawful to you, and your food is lawful to them—and the chaste ones from among faithful women, and chaste women of those who were given the Book before you, when you have given them their dowries, in wedlock, not in license, nor taking paramours. Should anyone renounce his faith, his work shall fail and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter. (5)
```

## (Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ** — "They ask you as to what is lawful for them. Say, ‘All the good things are allowed to you’" (5:4). This is an absolute question to which He responded with a general, absolute answer, providing a universal principle (الضابط الكلي) that distinguishes the lawful (الحلال) from the prohibited (الحرام). This principle is that whatever one intends to utilize, in the manner such things are customarily utilized, must be a "good thing" (أمراً طيباً). The absolute nature of the term "good things" (الطيب), without being restricted by any condition, implies that the criterion for identifying its "goodness" is what is deemed good by common understanding (استطابة الأفهام المتعارفة). Thus, whatever is considered good by ordinary human understanding is "good" (طيب), and everything that is "good" is lawful.

We have interpreted lawfulness (الحلية) and goodness (الطيب) according to common custom (المتعارف المعهود) because an absolute expression (الإطلاق) does not encompass anything else, as has been clarified in the science of legal theory (فن الأصول).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ فَكُلُوا مِمَّا أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ** — "and [lawful are] the hunting dogs and other animals that you have trained as hounds, teaching them out of what Allah has taught you. So eat of what they catch for you, and mention the name of Allah over it" (5:4). It has been said that this statement is coordinated (معطوف) with the position of "the good things" (الطيبات); meaning: "and lawful for you is what you have trained of the predatory animals"—that is, the prey of what you have trained. Thus, the speech assumes a deleted possessed noun (مضاف محذوف) for the sake of brevity, as the context indicates it.

However, it is more apparent that the sentence is coordinated with the position of the first sentence. The word "what" (ما) in His saying, "and what you have trained" (**وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ**), is a conditional particle (شرطية), and its apodosis (جزاؤها) is His saying, "so eat of what they catch for you" (**فَكُلُوا مِمَّا أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ**), without any need for the burden of assuming deleted words.

The "predatory animals" (الجوارح) is the plural of *jāriḥah* (جارحة), which refers to those animals that capture prey, whether birds or beasts of prey, such as the falcon (الصقر), the hawk (البازي), dogs (الكلاب), and cheetahs (الفهود). His saying, "as hounds" (**مُكَلِّبِينَ**), is a circumstantial qualifier (حال). The root of "training as hounds" (التكليب) is the training and raising of dogs for hunting, or the keeping of hunting dogs and sending them for that purpose. Restricting the sentence with the term *mukallibīn* is not devoid of an indication that the ruling is specific to the hunting dog (كلب الصيد) and does not extend to other predatory animals.

His saying, "of what they catch for you" (**مِمَّا أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ**): the restriction with the prepositional phrase is to indicate that the lawfulness is limited to the instance where it hunts for its owner, not for itself.

His saying, "and mention the name of Allah over it" (**وَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ**), is a completion of the conditions for lawfulness; namely, that the prey—despite being caught by predatory animals through the method of training as hounds and catching for the hunter—must have the name of Allah, the Exalted, mentioned over it.

The sum of the meaning is that predatory animals trained as hounds—meaning hunting dogs—if they are trained and catch for you any of the wild animals whose meat is lawful to eat through ritual slaughter (التذكية), and you have mentioned the name of Allah over it, then eat from it if it kills the prey before you can reach it, for that constitutes its ritual slaughter. As for if it does not kill it, then the ritual slaughter is by slaughtering (الذبح) and invoking the name of Allah (الإهلال) over it, which renders this specific ruling unnecessary.

Then He concluded the speech by saying: **وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ** — "and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning" (5:4). This is an intimation of the necessity of being God-conscious (اتقاء الله) regarding this matter, so that hunting does not become an excess in killing, nor done out of vanity or tyranny, as is the case with hunting for sport (صيد اللهو) and the like. For indeed, Allah is swift at reckoning; He punishes the evil of injustice and transgression in this world before the Hereafter. Such injustices and transgressions—committed through assassination and the slaughter of dumb animals—only lead to an evil end, as we have frequently witnessed.

His saying, the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ وَطَعَامُ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حِلٌّ لَكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَهُمْ** — "Today all the good things are allowed to you, and the food of those who were given the Book is allowed to you, and your food is allowed to them" (5:5). The repetition of the mention of the lawfulness of "the good things" (الطيبات), despite its mention in the previous verse, and its commencement with the word "Today" (**الْيَوْمَ**), is to indicate the favor (الامتنان) from Him, the Exalted, upon the believers by making the food of the People of the Book and the chaste women among them lawful for the believers.

The joining of His saying, "Today all the good things are allowed to you" (**أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ**), to His saying, "and the food of those who were given the Book" (**وَطَعَامُ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ**), etc., is of the nature of joining that which is certain (المقطوع به) to that which is doubted (المشكوك فيه) to create tranquility in the soul of the addressee and remove any anxiety or agitation. It is like a master saying to his servant: "You have all that I have given you, plus an increase which is such and such." If the servant doubts the realization of what his master promises to give, the master joins what is doubted with what is certain so that the pain of doubt leaves the servant's soul for the comfort of knowledge. From this perspective, one can interpret His saying, the Exalted: **لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنَى وَزِيَادَةٌ** — "For those who do good is the best [reward] and an extra" (10:26), and His saying: **لَهُمْ مَا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ** — "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35).

It was as if the souls of the believers would not rest from the agitation of doubt regarding the lawfulness of the food of the People of the Book for them, after they had witnessed the complete severity in social interaction, mixing, touching, and guardianship (الولاية) with them. Thus, the mention of the lawfulness of their food was joined with the lawfulness of "the good things" (الطيبات) in an absolute sense. They understood from this that their food is of the same genus as all other lawful good things, and thereby the flightiness of their souls subsided and their hearts found peace. The same applies to His saying: **وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنَاتِ وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** — "and the chaste women from among the faithful and the chaste women from among those who were given the Book before you" (5:5).

As for His saying, "and the food of those who were given the Book is allowed to you, and your food is allowed to them" (**وَطَعَامُ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حِلٌّ لَكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَهُمْ**), it is apparently a single statement with a single import. It is well known that His saying, "and your food is allowed to them," is not in the position of legislating the ruling of lawfulness for the People of the Book or directing a legal obligation (التكليف) toward them—even if we maintain that the disbelievers are obligated by the branches of religion (الفروع) just as they are by the roots (الأصول). For they do not believe in Allah, His Messenger, or what His Messenger brought, nor do they listen or accept. It is not the custom of the Quran to direct a discourse or mention a ruling if it appears from the context that the discourse with them would be futile (لغواً) and the speech would go to waste. Unless, of course, this is rectified by some rhetorical device, such as a transition (الالتفات) from addressing the people to addressing the Prophet, or the like, such as His saying: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا إِلَى كَلِمَةٍ سَوَاءٍ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you’" (3:64), and His saying: **قُلْ سُبْحَانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً** — "Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human messenger?’" (17:93), among other verses.

In short, the intent of His saying, "and the food of those..." is not to state the lawfulness of the food of the People of the Book for Muslims as one independent ruling and the lawfulness of the food of Muslims for the People of the Book as another independent ruling. Rather, it is to state a single ruling: the establishment of lawfulness and the removal of prohibition from the food. Thus, there is no barrier in between that would attach to either side, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ عَلِمْتُمُوهُنَّ مُؤْمِنَاتٍ فَلَا تَرْجِعُوهُنَّ إِلَى الْكُفَّارِ لَا هُنَّ حِلٌّ لَهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحِلُّونَ لَهُنَّ** — "then if you know them to be faithful, do not send them back to the faithless. They are not lawful for them, nor are they lawful for them" (60:10); meaning, there is no lawfulness in between that would attach to either side.

Furthermore, "food" (الطعام), according to its linguistic origin, is everything that is used as nourishment and eaten. However, it has been said that the intent here is wheat (البر) and other grains. In *Lisān al-ʿArab*: "When the people of the Hijaz use the word *al-ṭaʿām* absolutely, they mean wheat specifically." He said: "And al-Khalīl said: The superior usage in the speech of the Arabs is that *al-ṭaʿām* is wheat specifically." End quote.

This is also what appears from the words of Ibn al-Athīr in *al-Nihāyah*. For this reason, it has been narrated in most of the reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that the intent of "food" in the verse is wheat and other grains, except for what appears in some narrations suggesting another meaning, which will be discussed in the upcoming "Discussion of Narrations."

In any case, this lawfulness does not include what does not accept ritual slaughter (التذكية) from their food, such as pork, or what accepts it from their slaughtered animals but which they did not ritually slaughter—such as that over which the name of Allah was not invoked (لم يهل به لله) or which was not slaughtered according to Islamic ritual slaughter. For Allah, the Glorified, has counted these mentioned prohibitions in the verses of prohibition—which are the four verses in the chapters of *al-Baqarah*, *al-Māʾidah*, *al-Anʿām*, and *al-Naḥl*—as an abomination (رجساً), a transgression (فسقاً), and a sin (إثماً), as we have explained previously. Far be it from Him, the Glorified, to make lawful what He has called an abomination, transgression, or sin, while showing favor with a statement like: "Today all the good things are allowed to you."

Moreover, these very prohibitions occur just before this verse in the same Surah. No one can claim abrogation (النسخ) in such a case, as is obvious, especially in a Surah like *al-Māʾidah*, regarding which it has been narrated that it is abrogating (ناسخة) and not abrogated (منسوخة).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنَاتِ وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** — "and the chaste women from among the faithful and the chaste women from among those who were given the Book before you" (5:5). The use of the description "those who were given the Book" (**الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ**) as the object of the ruling, without saying "from the Jews and Christians" for example, or "from the People of the Book," is not devoid of an intimation of causality (العلية).

The tone is one of favor (الامتنان), and the context is one of alleviation (التخفيف) and facilitation (التسهيل). Thus, the meaning is: "We show favor to you by alleviating and facilitating the removal of the prohibition of marriage between your men and the chaste women among the People of the Book because they are closer to you than all other non-Muslim groups; they were given the Book and they acknowledge monotheism (التوحيد) and the Message, unlike the polytheists and idolaters who deny prophethood." What we have mentioned is also intimated by the restriction of His saying, "were given the Book," with His saying, "before you" (**مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ**), for in it is a clear intimation of mixing, blending, and partnership.

In any case, since the verse occurs in a context of favor and alleviation, it does not accept abrogation by verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَنْكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكَاتِ حَتَّى يُؤْمِنَّ** — "Do not marry polytheistic women until they believe" (2:221), and His saying: **وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا بِعِصَمِ الْكَوَافِرِ** — "and do not hold on to the ties of faithless women" (60:10), as is obvious.

Furthermore, the first verse is in Surah *al-Baqarah*, which is the first detailed Surah revealed in Medina before *al-Māʾidah*. Similarly, the second verse is in Surah *al-Mumtaḥanah*, which was revealed in Medina before the Conquest [of Mecca], so it too was revealed before *al-Māʾidah*. There is no reason for an earlier verse to abrogate a later one, in addition to what has been narrated: that *al-Māʾidah* was the last to be revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so it abrogated what was before it, and nothing abrogated it.

Moreover, you have already learned in the discussion of His saying, the Exalted: "Do not marry polytheistic women until they believe" (2:221) in the second volume of this book, that both verses—the verse of *al-Baqarah* and the verse of *al-Mumtaḥanah*—are irrelevant to the prohibition of marrying a woman of the People of the Book (الكتابية).

Even if it were said that the verse of *al-Mumtaḥanah* indicates prohibition in some way—just as the occurrence of the verse of *al-Māʾidah* in the context of favor and alleviation indicates a prior legal prohibition (for there is no favor or alleviation if no prohibition preceded it)—then the verse of *al-Māʾidah* would be the one abrogating the verse of *al-Mumtaḥanah*, not the reverse, because abrogation is the function of the later [revelation]. A discussion regarding the second verse will follow in the "Discussion of Narrations."

Furthermore, the intent of "chaste women" (المحصنات) in the verse is "the virtuous" (العفائف), which is one of the meanings of *iḥṣān* (إحصان). This is because His saying, "and the chaste women from among the faithful and the chaste women from among those who were given the Book," indicates that the intent of "chaste women" is other than married women, as is obvious. Then, the joining of the chaste women from the People of the Book and the believers—according to the clarification of its meaning—requires that the intent of "chaste women" in both places be a single meaning. It is not *iḥṣān* in the sense of Islam because of His saying: (**وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ**). Nor is the intent of "chaste women" "free women" (الحرائر), for the favor understood from the verse does not suit restricting the lawfulness to free women to the exclusion of slave women. Thus, no meaning of *iḥṣān* remains except chastity (العفة); therefore, it is determined that the intent of "chaste women" is "the virtuous" (العفائف).

After all this, the verse explicitly legislates the lawfulness of the chaste women of the People of the Book for the believers without restricting it to permanence (دوام) or temporality (انقطاع), except for what He mentioned regarding the condition of the dowry (الأجر) and that the enjoyment be in the manner of *iḥṣān* (chaste marriage), not in the manner of debauchery (المسافحة) or taking secret lovers (اتخاذ الأخدان). The result is that what has been made lawful for the believers regarding them is that it be by way of marriage with a dower and wage, not debauchery, without any other condition of permanent or temporary marriage. It has already been mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ** — "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries" (4:24) in the fourth volume of this book, that temporary marriage (*mutʿah*) is a marriage like permanent marriage. There are remaining points of discussion to be sought in the science of jurisprudence (علم الفقه).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِذَا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ وَلا مُتَّخِذِي أَخْدانٍ** — "once you have given them their dowries, being chaste and not committed to out-of-wedlock chastity, nor taking secret lovers" (5:5). This verse follows the course of His saying, the Exalted, in the verses of prohibited marriages: **وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ مَا وَرَاءَ ذَلِكُمْ أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوَالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَافِحِينَ** — "and lawful for you are [women] beyond these, that you seek [them] with your wealth, being chaste and not committed to out-of-wedlock chastity" (4:24). This sentence is a contextual clue (قرينة) that the intent of the verse is to state the lawfulness of marrying the chaste women of the People of the Book, without it including those possessed by the right hand (ملك اليمين).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ وَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ** — "Should anyone renounce the faith, his work shall fail and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter" (5:5). "Disbelief" (الكفر) in its origin means "to cover" (الستر). Thus, the realization of its concept depends on a fixed matter upon which the covering occurs, just as a veil (الحجاب) is not a veil unless there is something veiled. Therefore, disbelief requires a fixed object of disbelief, such as disbelief in the blessing of Allah, disbelief in the signs of Allah, and disbelief in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day.

Thus, "disbelief in faith" (الكفر بالإيمان) necessitates the existence of a fixed faith. The intent here is not the gerundial meaning (المعنى المصدري) of faith, but the meaning of the verbal noun (اسم المصدر)—which is the resulting effect and the fixed attribute in the heart of the believer; namely, the true beliefs (الاعتقادات الحقة) that are the source of righteous deeds. Thus, the meaning of "disbelief in faith" boils down to abandoning action according to what one knows to be the truth—such as taking the polytheists as guardians, mixing with them, and partnering in their deeds while knowing the truth of Islam; or abandoning the pillars of religion (الأركان الدينية) like prayer, zakat, fasting, and Hajj while knowing their status as pillars of the religion.

This is the intent of "disbelief in faith." However, there is a subtle point here: since disbelief is a "covering," and the covering of fixed matters is not realized—according to what first comes to mind—except with persistence and practice, "disbelief in faith" is only realized when a person abandons acting upon what his faith requires and what his knowledge relates to, and persists in that. As for if he covers it once or twice without persisting in it, it is not called "disbelief" (كفر); rather, it is a transgression (فسق) he has committed.

From here it becomes clear that the intent of His saying, "And whoever disbelieves in faith" (**وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ**), is persistence and continuity in it, even though He expressed it with a verb rather than a description. Thus, the one who abandons following what has been established as truth with him, and what has been fixed with him of the pillars of religion, is a disbeliever in faith, whose work has failed, as He said: "his work shall fail" (**فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ**).

The verse thus corresponds to His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الرُّشْدِ لَا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الْغَيِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَكَانُوا عَنْهَا غَافِلِينَ وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَلِقَاءِ الْآخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "If they see the way of rectitude, they will not take it as [their] way, and if they see the way of error, they will take it as [their] way. That is because they denied Our signs and were oblivious of them. As for those who deny Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, their works have failed. Shall they be requited except for what they used to do?" (7:146-147). He described them as taking the way of error and abandoning the way of rectitude after seeing both—which is knowledge of them—then He replaced that by describing them as denying the signs. A sign only becomes a sign after knowledge of its indication. Then He explained it as denying the Hereafter, because if the Hereafter were not denied, knowledge of it would prevent the abandonment of truth. Then He informed of the failure of their works.

Similar to this is His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالاً الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعاً أُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَائِهِ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فَلَا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَزْناً** — "Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? Those whose efforts go astray in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good.’ They are the ones who deny the signs of their Lord and the encounter with Him. So their works have failed, then on the Day of Resurrection We will not set up any balance for them" (18:103-105). The correspondence of these verses to the case of "disbelief in faith" in the sense previously explained is clear.

By reflecting on what we have mentioned, the reason for the connection of the sentence—namely His saying: "And whoever disbelieves in faith, his work shall fail"—to what precedes it becomes clear. The sentence completes the previous explanation and is in the position of warning against the danger that could face the believers by being negligent in the matter of Allah and being overly permissive with the disbelievers. For Allah, the Glorified, only made the food of the People of the Book and their chaste women lawful for the believers to be an alleviation and facilitation from Him for them, and a means for the spread of the word of God-consciousness (التقوى) and the transmission of pure Islamic character from the Muslims who embody it to others, thereby being a caller to beneficial knowledge and an incentive toward righteous deeds.

This is the purpose of the legislation; it is not so that it be taken as a means to fall into the depths of desire, to ascend into the valleys of whims, and to be unrestrained in love and infatuation for them, and bewitched by their beauty. For then they would become role models whose character and the character of their people would dominate the character of the Muslims, and their corruption would overcome the Muslims' righteousness. Then the trial would occur, and the believers would turn back on their heels, and the end result would be that this divine favor turns into a trial and a destructive ordeal, and this alleviation, which is a blessing, becomes a curse.

Thus, Allah warned the believers after explaining the lawfulness of their food and their chaste women not to be unrestrained in enjoying this blessing in a way that leads to disbelief in faith, the abandonment of the pillars of religion, and turning away from the truth; for that necessitates the failure of work and leads to the loss of effort in the Hereafter.

Know that the commentators have delved deeply into this verse—namely His saying: "Today all the good things are allowed to you" (to the end of the verse)—which led them to strange interpretations that the literal wording does not support and the context of the verse contradicts. Such as the saying of some: that His saying, "all the good things are allowed to you," means food such as the *baḥīrah*, *sāʾibah*, *waṣīlah*, and *ḥām* [pagan animal categories]. And the saying of some: that His saying, "and the food of those who were given the Book is allowed to you," means that according to the primary origin, Allah never prohibited it to you, and that meat is among the lawful even if they did not ritually slaughter it except by what they have of ritual slaughter. And the saying of some: that the intent of His saying, "and the food of those..." is eating with them. And the saying of some: that the intent of His saying, "and the chaste women from among the faithful and the chaste women from among those who were given the Book before you," is a statement of lawfulness according to the origin without it being prohibited before that; rather, His saying, the Exalted: "and lawful for you are [women] beyond these" (4:24) is sufficient in making them lawful. And the saying of some: that the intent of His saying, "And whoever disbelieves in faith, his work shall fail," is a warning against rejecting the matter of the lawfulness of the food of the People of the Book and their chaste women mentioned at the beginning of the verse.

These and similar meanings are possibilities they entertained, and they range between what is not devoid of rashness and arbitrariness—such as restricting His saying, "Today... are allowed," to what preceded without evidence—and what is refuted by the apparent context of the restriction to "Today," the favor, the alleviation, and other things previously explained. The previous explanation, in which we relied on the apparent meanings of the noble verses, is sufficient to invalidate them and demonstrate the aspect of their flaw.

As for the claim that the verse "and lawful for you are [women] beyond these" indicates the lawfulness of marrying a woman of the People of the Book, its invalidity is manifest, as the verse is clearly in the position of stating the prohibited and permitted women according to the categories of lineage (نسب) and cause (سبب), not according to the categories of religions and creeds.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ** (the verse): Ibn Jarīr narrated from ʿIkrimah: "That the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent Abū Rāfiʿ to kill dogs—so he killed until he reached al-ʿAwālī. Then ʿĀṣim ibn ʿAdī, Saʿd ibn Khaythamah, and ʿUwaym ibn Sāʿidah entered and said: 'What has been made lawful for us, O Messenger of Allah?' So it was revealed: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ** (the verse)."

And in it, Ibn Jarīr narrated from Muḥammad ibn Kaʿb al-Quraẓī, who said: "When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered the killing of dogs, they said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is lawful for us from this nation (الأمة)?' So it was revealed: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ** (the verse)."

I say: The two narrations explain each other. The intent is the question regarding what is lawful for them of dogs in terms of keeping them and using them for various needs like hunting and the like. However, His saying, the Exalted: "They ask you as to what is lawful for them. Say, ‘All the good things are allowed to you’" does not suit this meaning because of its restriction and the absolute nature of the verse.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of the two narrations and the upcoming narration is that His saying: "and what you have trained of the predatory animals" (**وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ**) is coordinated with the position of "the good things" (الطيبات), and the meaning is: "and lawful for you is what you have trained." For this reason, a group of commentators committed themselves to assuming deleted words, as mentioned before. But it has already been stated that the apparent meaning is that His saying, "and what you have trained," is a condition whose apodosis is His saying: "so eat of what they catch for you."

The "nation" (الأمة) asked about in the narration refers to the species of dogs, as explained by the upcoming narration.

And in it, al-Fāryābī, Ibn Jarīr, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abī Ḥātim, al-Ṭabarānī, al-Ḥākim—who authenticated it—and al-Bayhaqī in his *Sunan* narrated from Abū Rāfiʿ, who said: "Gabriel came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and asked permission to enter—so he permitted him, but he delayed. So he took his cloak and went out—and said: 'We have permitted you.' He said: 'Yes, but we do not enter a house in which there is a dog or a picture.' So they looked and found a puppy in one of their houses."

Abū Rāfiʿ said: "So he ordered me to kill every dog in Medina, and I did so. Then the people came and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is lawful for us from this nation that you ordered to be killed?' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) remained silent, so Allah revealed: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ** — so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When a man sends his dog and mentions the name of Allah—and it catches for him, let him eat what it has not eaten from.'"

I say: What is mentioned in the narration regarding the manner of Gabriel's descent is strange in its kind. Moreover, the narration is not devoid of agitation (اضطراب), as it indicates Gabriel's refraining from entering upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) due to the presence of a puppy in one of their houses. Furthermore, it does not correspond to the apparent meaning of the verse in terms of the absolute nature of the question and answer, and the coordination in His saying (**وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ**). Thus, the narration is more akin to being fabricated (الموضوعة).

And in it, ʿAbd ibn Ḥumayd and Ibn Jarīr narrated from ʿĀmir: "That ʿAdī ibn Ḥātim al-Ṭāʾī came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and asked him about hunting with dogs, and he did not know what to say to him—until Allah revealed to him this verse in *al-Māʾidah*—(**تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ**)."

I say: There are other reports with the same meaning, and the previous problem applies to it. It appears that these narrations and those like them are an application (تطبيق) of events to the verse, except that it is an incomplete application. It is more likely that they mentioned hunting with dogs to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), then they asked him for a universal principle (ضابط كلي) to distinguish the lawful from the prohibited. So their question was mentioned in the verse, then it was answered by providing the universal principle in His saying: (**يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ**), then they were answered regarding the specific matter they discussed. This is what the tone of the speech in the verse suggests.

In *al-Kāfī*, with its chain of transmission from Ḥammād, from al-Ḥalabī, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "In the book of ʿAlī (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—(**وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ**) — he said: 'They are dogs.'"

I say: And al-ʿAyyāshī narrated it in his *Tafsīr* from Samāʿah ibn Mihrān from him (peace be upon him).

And in it, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Muskān, from al-Ḥalabī, who said: "Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) said: 'My father used to give fatwas and he used to practice precautionary dissimulation (يتقي)—and we used to fear regarding hunting with hawks and falcons. But now we do not fear, and hunting with them is not lawful—unless you reach it in time for ritual slaughter. For it is in the book of ʿAlī (peace be upon him) that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ** regarding dogs.'"

And in it, with its chain of transmission from Abū Bakr al-Ḥaḍramī, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about hunting with hawks, falcons, cheetahs, and dogs—he said: 'Do not eat except what you ritually slaughter, except for dogs.' I said: 'What if it kills it?' He said: 'Eat, for Allah says: **وَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ فَكُلُوا مِمَّا أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ**.' Then he said: 'Every beast of prey catches the prey for itself—except for trained dogs; for they catch for their owner.' He said: 'And when you send the dog, mention the name of Allah over it, for that is its ritual slaughter.'"

In *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī*, from Abū ʿUbaydah, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him): "Regarding a man who sends a trained dog and mentions the name of Allah when he sends it. He said: 'He may eat of what they catch for him even if it reaches it and kills it. But if he finds another untrained dog with it, do not eat from it.' I said: 'What about falcons, eagles, and hawks?' He said: 'If you reach it in time for ritual slaughter, eat from it; but if you do not reach it in time for ritual slaughter, do not eat from it.' I said: 'Is the cheetah not in the same position as the dog?' He said: 'No, nothing is *mukallab* (trained as a hound) except the dog.'"

And in it, from Abū Baṣīr, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him): "Regarding the saying of Allah: **مَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ فَكُلُوا مِمَّا أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ** — he said: 'There is no harm in eating what the dog catches—provided the dog has not eaten from it—so if the dog eats from it before you reach it, do not eat it.'"

I say: The specific details taken in the narrations—such as the restriction of lawfulness upon killing to the prey of the dog because of His saying: "as hounds" (**مُكَلِّبِينَ**) and His saying: "of what they catch for you" (**مِمَّا أَمْسكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ**), and the condition that an untrained dog does not participate with it—all of that is derived from the verse. Some discussion on this has already preceded.

And in it, from Ḥarīz, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him), who said: "He was asked about the dog of a Magian (المجوس) which a Muslim trains, mentions the name of Allah, and sends—he said: 'Yes, it is *mukallab* (trained as a hound) when the name of Allah is mentioned over it, so there is no harm.'"

I say: In this is the taking of the absolute nature of His saying "as hounds" (**مُكَلِّبِينَ**). It has been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthūr* from Ibn Abī Ḥātim from Ibn ʿAbbās: "Regarding a Muslim who takes the trained dog of a Magian, or his hawk, or his falcon—from what the Magian trained—and sends it and it catches—he said: 'Do not eat it even if you mention the name of Allah—because it is from the training of the Magian, and He only said: **تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ**.'" Its weakness is manifest, for although the address in His saying "out of what Allah has taught you" (**مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ**) is apparently directed to the believers, that which Allah taught them of what they teach the dogs is nothing other than what Allah taught the Magians and others. This meaning supports the listener's understanding that there is no specificity to the training of a believer insofar as it is the training of a believer; thus, there is no difference in the trained dog whether its trainer is a Muslim or a non-Muslim, just as there is no difference in terms of ownership whether it is owned by a Muslim or a non-Muslim.

In *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī*, from Hishām ibn Sālim, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him): "Regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: 'and their food is allowed to you'—he said: 'Lentils, grains, and the like' (meaning the People of the Book)." I say: And it was narrated in *al-Tahdhīb* from him, and its wording is: "He said: 'Lentils, chickpeas, and other than that.'" In *al-Kāfī* and *al-Tahdhīb*, in narrations from ʿAmmār ibn Marwān and Samāʿah, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him): "Regarding the food of the People of the Book and what is lawful of it, he said: 'Grains.'" In *al-Kāfī*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Muskān, from Qutaybah al-Aʿshā, who said: "A man asked Abū ʿAbdillāh while I was with him, saying to him: 'A Jew or a Christian is sent with the sheep—and an accident occurs to one of them so he slaughters it; may his slaughtered animal be eaten?' Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) said: 'Do not enter its price into your wealth and do not eat it—for it is only the Name, and no one is trusted over it except a Muslim.' The man said to him: 'Allah the Exalted said: **الْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ وَطَعَامُ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حِلٌّ لَكُمْ**.' So Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) said: 'My father used to say: It is only grains and the like.'"

I say: And it was narrated by the Shaykh in *al-Tahdhīb* and al-ʿAyyāshī in his *Tafsīr* from Qutaybah al-Aʿshā from him (peace be upon him).

The traditions—as you see—interpret the lawful food of the People of the Book in the verse as grains and the like. This is what the word "food" (الطعام) indicates when used absolutely, as is apparent from the narrations and stories transmitted from the early period. For this reason, the majority of our scholars have gone toward restricting the lawfulness to grains, their likes, and what is made from them of what is used for nourishment.

Some (1) have severely criticized them, claiming that this contradicts the custom of the Quran in the usage of "food."

He said: "This is not the predominant usage in the language of the Quran. For Allah the Exalted said in this Surah—meaning *al-Māʾidah*: **أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهُ مَتَاعاً لَكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ** — 'Lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you and for the travellers' (5:96), and no one says that the food from the game of the sea is wheat or grains. And He said: **كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلّاً لِبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَى نَفْسِهِ** — 'All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden to himself' (3:93), and no one said that the food here is wheat or grain absolutely, since nothing of it was prohibited to the Children of Israel either before the Torah or after it. Thus, food in its origin is everything that is tasted (يطعم), i.e., tasted or eaten. Allah the Exalted said regarding the water of the river, narrating from Saul: **فَمَنْ شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّي وَمَنْ لَمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي** — 'Whoever drinks from it does not belong to me, and whoever does not taste it does belong to me' (2:249), and He said: **فَإِذَا طَعِمْتُمْ فَانْتَشِرُوا** — 'and when you have eaten, disperse' (33:53), meaning eaten."

I wish I knew what he understood from their saying: "Food, when used absolutely, refers to grains and the like." For he did not hesitate to object to them with examples like "tastes it" (**يَطْعَمُهُ**) and "you have eaten" (**طَعِمْتُمْ**) from the derivatives of the verb! They only said what they said regarding the noun "food" (الطعام), not regarding the verbs formed from it. And he objected with examples like "food of the sea" (**طَعَامُ الْبَحْرِ**), where the genitive construction (الإضافة) is the clearest contextual clue, for neither wheat nor barley grows in the sea. And he objected with examples like: "All food was lawful to the Children of Israel," then he himself mentioned that it is known from their religion that wheat or grain was not prohibited to them. He ought to have reviewed the instances in the Quran where the word is used absolutely, and then said what he had to say, such as His saying: **فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ** — "a redemption by feeding a needy person" (2:184), and His saying: **أَوْ كَفَّارَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسَاكِينَ** — "or an atonement by feeding needy persons" (5:95), and His saying: **وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعَامَ** — "and give food" (76:8), and His saying: **فَلْيَنْظُرِ الْإِنْسَانُ إِلَى طَعَامِهِ** — "Let man then consider his food" (80:24), and the like.

Then he said: "Grain is not a likely subject for lawfulness and prohibition; rather, it is meat that is subject to that due to a sensory attribute, such as the animal dying naturally, or a conceptual one, such as seeking nearness to other than Allah. For this reason, Allah the Exalted said: **قُلْ لَا أَجِدُ فِي مَا أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلَى طَاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَماً مَسْفُوحاً** — 'Say, "I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to an eater who eats it, unless it be carrion, or spilt blood..."' (6:145). All of this relates to animals, and it is an explicit text in restricting prohibition to what was mentioned; thus, the prohibition of anything else requires an explicit text."

This speech of his is even stranger than the previous one. As for his saying: "Grain is not a likely subject for lawfulness and prohibition; rather, it is meat that is subject to that," it should be said to him: In what time do you mean that? In times like these, when minds have become accustomed to Islam and its general rulings for several centuries? Or at the time of revelation, when only a few years of the religion's life had passed? They asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about things that are clearer than the ruling on grains and their likes, and more manifest. Allah the Exalted narrated some of that, as in His saying: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنْفِقُونَ** — "They ask you as to what they should spend" (2:215). ʿAbd ibn Ḥumayd narrated from Qatādah, who said: "It was mentioned to us that some men said: 'How can we marry their women while they are on one religion and we are on another?'—so Allah revealed: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ** (the narration)." Similar examples of this saying have passed and will come in the folds of the narrations, as we have cited regarding the *Ḥajj al-Tamattuʿ* and other matters.

If they were saying such things after the revelation of the verse regarding the lawfulness of the chaste women of the People of the Book, what would prevent them from asking, before the revelation of the verse, about eating with the People of the Book, and eating what is taken from them of grains and the foods made from them—such as bread, *harīsah*, and all other foods made from grains and the like—if the People of the Book prepared them, while they are on one religion and we are on another? Especially since Allah had warned the believers against loving them, taking them as guardians, approaching them, and relying on them in many verses.

Rather, this argument is turned against him in his saying that meat is the likely subject for prohibition and lawfulness. How could they possibly ask about it when Allah had explained all the prohibited meats in the verse of *al-Anʿām*: "Say, 'I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to an eater who eats it...'" (6:145), then in the verse of *al-Naḥl*—both of which are Meccan—then in the verse of *al-Baqarah*, which was revealed before *al-Māʾidah*, then in His saying: "Forbidden to you is carrion," which is before this verse? The verse, according to this speaker's view, is an explicit text or like an explicit text in the non-prohibition of their slaughtered animals. So how was it correct for these people to ask about the lawfulness of the slaughtered animals of the People of the Book when the verses—Meccan and Medinan—had been revealed time after time regarding them and indicated their lawfulness, and the practice had been established on preserving, reciting, learning, and acting upon them?

As for his saying that the verse of *al-Anʿām* is an explicit text in restricting the prohibitions to what is mentioned in it, so the prohibition of anything else—like the slaughtered animal of the People of the Book—requires evidence; there is no doubt that every ruling requires evidence to support it. This speech of his is an explicit admission that this restriction only benefits if there is no evidence to support the prohibition of another matter beyond what is mentioned in the verse.

Accordingly, if his intent by "evidence" is what includes the Sunnah, then the one who maintains the prohibition of the slaughtered animals of the People of the Book relies on what has been narrated of reports regarding the verse, and we have cited some of them previously.

If he intends evidence from the Book—notwithstanding that this is an arbitrary claim without evidence, since the Sunnah is the partner of the Book and they do not diverge in authority—he should be asked: What does he say regarding the slaughtered animals of the disbelievers other than the People of the Book, such as idolaters and materialists? Does he prohibit them because they are carrion (ميتة) lacking Islamic ritual slaughter? What is the difference between the lack of ritual slaughter due to not facing the Qiblah and not mentioning the name of Allah at all, and the ritual slaughter which is other than Islamic ritual slaughter and which Allah the Glorified does not approve of and has abrogated? All of them are "filthy things" (خبائث) in the view of the religion, and Allah has prohibited filthy things. He, the Exalted, said: **وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبَائِثَ** — "and makes lawful for them all the good things and forbids them all the foul things" (7:157). And Allah the Exalted said in the verse preceding this one: "They ask you as to what is lawful for them. Say, ‘All the good things are allowed to you’" (5:4). The tone of the question and answer in it is the clearest evidence for restricting lawfulness to "the good things" (الطيبات). Likewise, what is at the beginning of this verse: "Today all the good things are allowed to you," and the context of favor, indicates the aforementioned restriction.

If the prohibition of the slaughtered animals of the disbelievers is because they are slaughtered with the invocation of other than Allah—such as slaughtering in the name of idols—the argument returns to the lack of difference between invoking other than Allah and invoking Allah according to an abrogated method that Allah the Glorified does not approve of.

Then he said: "Allah was severe regarding what the Arab polytheists were upon of eating carrion of the aforementioned types and slaughtering for idols, so that the early Muslims would not be negligent in it following habit; and the People of the Book were further than them from eating carrion and slaughtering for idols."

He has forgotten that the Christians among the People of the Book eat pork, and Allah the Exalted mentioned it and was severe regarding it; and that they eat everything that the polytheists permit because the prohibition was lifted from them through the atonement (التفدية).

Furthermore, this is a trivial juristic preference (استحسان) that is of no use and cannot be relied upon in interpreting the word of Allah, understanding the meanings of His verses, or in the jurisprudence of the rulings of His religion.

Then he said: "And because it was part of the policy of the religion to be severe in dealing with the Arab polytheists so that no one remains in the Peninsula except that he enters Islam, and He alleviated the dealing with the People of the Book." Then he mentioned instances of the fatwas of some companions regarding the lawfulness of what they slaughtered for churches and so on.

This speech of his is based on what appears from some narrations that Allah chose the Arabs over other nations, and that they have a dignity over others. For this reason, they used to call others *al-mawālī* (clients). This does not suit the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses. Allah the Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثَى وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ أَتْقَاكُمْ** — "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13). And from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) are many traditions with this meaning.

Islam, in its call, did not place the Arabs on one side and others on another side. Rather, it only placed the non-People of the Book among the polytheists—whether they were Arabs or others—on one side, not accepting from them anything except that they submit and believe; and the People of the Book—whether they were Arabs or others—on another side, accepting from them entry into the protected status (الذمة) and the payment of the tribute (الجزية) unless they submit.

This point, even if granted, does not indicate more than a general permissiveness toward them due to its ambiguity. As for the claim that it must be by permitting their slaughtered animals if they slaughter them according to their own way and custom, where is the evidence for that? This is manifest.

As for what he mentioned of the practice of some companions and their sayings, etc., there is no authority in it.

Thus, it has become clear from all that has preceded that neither the verse nor any other evidence indicates the lawfulness of the slaughtered animals of the People of the Book if they are slaughtered without Islamic ritual slaughter. If we maintain the lawfulness of their slaughtered animals due to the verse—as has been transmitted from some of our companions—then let us restrict it to when it is known that the slaughter occurred according to legal ritual slaughter, as appears from the saying of al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) in the report of *al-Kāfī* and *al-Tahdhīb* mentioned earlier: "for it is only the Name, and no one is trusted over it except a Muslim" (the narration). There is a completion to this discussion to be sought in jurisprudence.

In *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī*, from al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him): "Regarding the saying of Allah the Exalted: **وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** (the verse)—he said: 'They are the virtuous (العفائف).'" And in it, from him (peace be upon him): "Regarding His saying: **وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنَاتِ** (the verse)—he said: 'They are the Muslim women.'"

In *Tafsīr al-Qummī*, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Marriage is only lawful with the People of the Book who pay the tribute (الجزية); as for others, marriage with them is not lawful."

I say: That is because they are combatants (محاربين) in that case.

In *al-Kāfī* and *al-Tahdhīb*, from al-Bāqir (peace be upon him): "Marriage is only lawful with the simple-minded (البله) among them."

In *al-Faqīh*, from al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him): "Regarding a believing man who marries a Christian or Jewish woman—he said: 'If he finds a Muslim woman, what would he do with a Jewess or a Christian?' It was said: 'He may have a desire for her.' He said: 'If he does, let him prevent her from drinking wine and eating pork—and know that there is a deficiency (غضاضة) in his religion because of it.'" In *al-Tahdhīb*, from al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him), who said: "There is no harm in a man performing temporary marriage (*mutʿah*) with a Jewess or a Christian—while he has a free wife."

In *al-Faqīh*, from al-Bāqir (peace be upon him): "That he was asked about a Muslim man marrying a Magian woman. He said: 'No, but if he has a Magian slave woman, there is no harm in him having intercourse with her, practicing withdrawal (يعزل) from her, and not seeking a child from her.'"

In *al-Kāfī*, with its chain of transmission from ʿAbdullāh ibn Sinān, from Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) in a narration: "He said: 'I do not like for a Muslim man to marry a Jewess or a Christian—for fear that his child might become a Jew or a Christian.'"

In *al-Kāfī*, with its chain of transmission from Zurārah, and in *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī*, from Masʿadah ibn Ṣadaqah, who said: "I asked Abū Jaʿfar (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah the Exalted: **وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** — so he said: 'It is abrogated by His saying: **وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا بِعِصَمِ الْكَوَافِرِ**.'"

I say: This is problematic because the revelation of His saying "and do not hold on to..." (the verse) preceded His saying "and the chaste women..." (the verse), and it is not permissible for the abrogating (الناسخ) to precede the abrogated (المنسوخ). In addition to what has been narrated that Surah *al-Māʾidah* is abrogating and not abrogated, and the discussion on this has already preceded. Evidence that the verse is not abrogated is the previous narration indicating the permissibility of temporary marriage with a woman of the People of the Book, and the companions have acted upon it; and it has already been mentioned in the verse of *mutʿah* that temporary marriage is a marriage and a wedding.

Yes, if it were said that His saying "and do not hold on to the ties of faithless women" (the verse) is a preceding specificator (مخصص متقدم) by which permanent marriage is excluded from the absolute nature of His saying "and the chaste women from among those who were given the Book before you," due to its indication of the prohibition of holding on to the tie (العصمة), which applies to permanent marriage just as it applies to maintaining the tie of marriage after the husband's conversion to Islam—which is the occasion of the verse's revelation.

One should not listen to the one who objects to this by saying that the verse was revealed regarding the husband's conversion to Islam while the wife remains in disbelief; for the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) does not restrict the word in its apparent meaning. It has already been mentioned in the interpretation of the verse of abrogation in Surah *al-Baqarah* in the first volume of this book that abrogation (النسخ) in the custom of the Quran and according to the origin encompasses things other than technical abrogation, such as specification (التخصيص).

In some narrations, it is also said that the verse is abrogated by His saying: "Do not marry polytheistic women" (the verse), and the problem with this has already preceded. There is a completion to this discussion to be sought in jurisprudence.

In *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ** (the verse): from Abān ibn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān, who said: "I heard Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) saying: 'The least by which a man exits Islam is that he holds an opinion contrary to the truth and persists in it.' He said: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ**." And he (peace be upon him) said: "The one who disbelieves in faith is the one who does not act upon what Allah has commanded and is not pleased with it."

And in it, from Muḥammad ibn Muslim, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them), who said: "It is the abandonment of action until he leaves it entirely."

I say: What clarifies the specific details of interpretation in these reports has already preceded.

And in it, from ʿUbayd ibn Zurārah, who said: "I asked Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ** — he said: 'Abandoning the action that he acknowledged; among that is that he abandons the prayer without illness or preoccupation.'"

I say: Allah the Exalted called the prayer "faith" (إيماناً) in His saying: **وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَانَكُمْ** — "Allah would not let your faith go to waste" (2:143), and perhaps he (peace be upon him) specified it for mention for that reason.

In *Tafsīr al-Qummī*, he (peace be upon him) said: "Whoever believes and then obeys the people of polytheism." And in *al-Baṣāʾir*, from Abū Ḥamzah, who said: "I asked Abū Jaʿfar (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ وَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ** — he said: 'Its interpretation in the inner depth (بطن) of the Quran is: Whoever disbelieves in the guardianship (الولاية) of ʿAlī. And ʿAlī is the Faith.'"

I say: This is from the inner meaning (البطن) as opposed to the outward meaning (الظهر), in the sense that we explained in the discussion on the clear (المحكم) and the ambiguous (المتشابه) in the third volume of this book. It is also possible that this is a case of applying a general concept to a specific instance (الجري والتطبيق). Indeed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله named ‘Ali عليه‌السلام "Faith" (إيمانا) when he went out to face ‘Amr ibn ‘Abd Wudd on the Day of the Trench (يوم الخندق), where he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **برز الإيمان كله إلى الكفر كله** — "All of Faith has gone out to face all of Disbelief."

There are other narrations that carry this same meaning.
